content.text: A promotion gate is an automated quality checkpoint that must pass before a build artifact advances from one deployment environment to the next. Rather than rebuilding code at each environment boundary, the gate evaluates the
— the exact binary, container image, or packaged prompt bundle produced by the initial build — and decides whether it is safe to carry forward.

In conventional software pipelines, gates typically check test pass rates, security scan results, and performance benchmarks. For agentic AI services the gate surface expands: an artifact carries not just application code but also system prompts, model version references, and tool configuration. Each of these can introduce regressions independent of code changes, so promotion gates must cover the full agentic assembly.

Gates are defined as code — usually YAML files stored alongside the pipeline definition — and executed by the CI/CD platform. A blocking gate stops the pipeline entirely until the condition passes or is explicitly overridden. A non-blocking gate records a warning but allows promotion to continue, typically used for low-severity checks during early project phases.

Without promotion gates, the only way to detect that a new agent build is worse than its predecessor is to expose real users to it. Gates move that detection earlier, at the staging boundary, where failures are cheaper to fix and carry no user impact. For LLM-backed systems this is especially important because regression can be subtle: a change to the system prompt may shift the agent's tone without breaking any code path, yet significantly degrade user satisfaction scores on the eval suite.

Gates also establish a clear audit record. Every promotion that reaches production passed a documented set of quality conditions at a recorded point in time. When an incident occurs, the audit log answers: what was the promotion gate state when this build was approved? Which eval categories passed and at what thresholds? Was any gate bypassed, and by whom?
Catching a failing eval gate at the staging boundary is orders of magnitude cheaper than a production rollback.
Every build is evaluated against the same thresholds, removing manual judgment variation from the promotion decision.
Gate results and bypass events are stored alongside the artifact metadata, supporting post-incident review and compliance reporting.
Engineers can iterate on prompts or model configuration knowing that any regression strong enough to break a gate will be caught before it reaches users.
Fewer production incidents from bad deployments means less time spent on incident response and post-mortems.
Multiple feature branches can run their gates concurrently without interfering, since each gate evaluates an isolated artifact.

Gates introduce pipeline latency. An eval gate that runs 500 test cases against a live model adds real wall-clock time between commit and deploy. Teams must balance gate thoroughness against deployment velocity, often tiering gate suites by environment: fast, lightweight checks at the dev boundary and more expensive eval suites only at the staging-to-production boundary.
Setting an eval pass-rate threshold too high blocks legitimate improvements; too low, and it fails to catch real regressions. Thresholds must be revisited as the eval suite and model evolve.
Running a large eval suite at each gate boundary incurs LLM API costs. At scale, this is a meaningful line item that must be budgeted and tracked.
False confidence from a narrow eval suite.
A gate that only measures one dimension — accuracy on a golden dataset — will pass builds that have regressed on latency, toxicity, or tool-call reliability.
If bypass is too easy, gates are only as effective as the culture around using them. Bypass events must be logged, reviewed, and minimized.
A promotion gate has four components: a
(what value must be met?), and a

The pipeline first builds the artifact and assigns it a content-addressed identifier — a SHA256 digest for a container image, or a semantic version tag for a prompt bundle. Subsequent pipeline stages run checks

. If all configured checks for a given environment boundary pass, the pipeline promotes the artifact by updating a pointer (a Kubernetes image tag, a manifest file in the GitOps repo, or an environment variable in the deployment system). If any check fails, the artifact is held and the pipeline reports the failure with the specific gate that blocked it.

attrs.alt: Artifact promotion pipeline: Build stage produces a SHA256-tagged artifact, Gate 1 (unit tests, lint, security scan) allows promotion to Staging, Gate 2 (eval pass rate, latency, regression check) allows promotion to Production. Failed gates route to a blocked artifact holding area.

content.text: The diagram shows three promotion boundaries. The build stage produces a tagged artifact.
at the dev boundary runs fast automated checks: unit tests, linting, type checking, and a vulnerability scan. If it passes, the artifact is deployed to the staging environment.

at the staging-to-production boundary runs the expensive checks: the full eval suite (pass-rate threshold), latency benchmarks (p95 SLA), and a regression comparison against the baseline. An optional manual approval gate can sit between Gate 2 and production for high-stakes deployments.
Unit and integration test pass rate.
Require 100% of unit tests to pass; integration tests at some configurable threshold (e.g., 98%).
The agent runs a curated golden dataset of inputs. The fraction of correct outputs must exceed a threshold (e.g., 90%) and must not be more than 2% below the previous deployed version's rate.
p95 time-to-first-token or end-to-end response latency must remain below the SLA boundary.
Container image scans (Trivy, Grype) must report no critical CVEs; SAST tools must find no new high-severity findings.
A battery of adversarial inputs exercises the agent's jailbreak resistance. Any new failure is a gate block.
Human sign-off is required before production deployment. GitHub Environments and Jenkins Input steps both provide native support for pausing a pipeline pending an approval action.
The approval UI presents the diff between the current production artifact and the candidate, the eval report, and a summary of any non-blocking warnings.
SLA timers (e.g., 4-hour auto-expiry) prevent approvals from dangling indefinitely.
at every staging-to-production boundary for any agent that takes user-facing actions.
during early project phases when the eval suite is still being calibrated and thresholds are not yet stable.
for agents with write access to production data sources, payment systems, or external APIs.
that measure things you cannot reliably compute. A gate on "user satisfaction" backed by heuristics you do not trust will block good builds and pass bad ones.
on internal tooling agents with no external impact and rapid rollback capability. Apply proportionate gating.
) in version control alongside the pipeline definition.
Tag every build artifact with a content-addressed digest (
) and semantic version at build time.
Write a gate runner script that reads the gate config, runs the eval suite, compares results to thresholds, and exits non-zero on failure.
Add the gate runner as a required CI job that must pass before the deploy job can execute.
Store gate results (pass/fail, metric values, threshold at time of check) in the artifact metadata store for audit purposes.
Implement a documented bypass procedure: require an override comment in the PR, notify the on-call channel, and log the bypass with approver identity.

# .github/workflows/promote-to-staging.yml
name: Promote to Staging

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      image-digest: ${{ steps.build.outputs.digest }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Build and push container
        id: build
        uses: docker/build-push-action@v5
        with:
          push: true
          tags: ghcr.io/myorg/agent:${{ github.sha }}

  gate-dev:
    needs: build
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Run unit tests
        run: pytest tests/unit/ --tb=short -q
      - name: Lint and type check
        run: |
          ruff check .
          mypy src/
      - name: Security scan
        uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@master
        with:
          image-ref: ghcr.io/myorg/agent@${{ needs.build.outputs.image-digest }}
          exit-code: '1'
          severity: 'CRITICAL'

  promote-to-staging:
    needs: [build, gate-dev]
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Update staging manifest
        run: |
          # Update the image digest in the GitOps repo
          sed -i 's|image:.*|image: ghcr.io/myorg/agent@${{ needs.build.outputs.image-digest }}|' \
            deploy/staging/deployment.yaml
          git commit -am "promote: ${{ github.sha }} → staging"
          git push

# gates.yaml — version-controlled gate definitions
version: "1.0"

environments:
  dev-to-staging:
    blocking:
      - name: unit_tests
        check: test_pass_rate
        threshold: 1.0          # 100% of unit tests must pass
      - name: type_check
        check: mypy_errors
        threshold: 0            # zero type errors
      - name: security_scan
        check: critical_cves
        threshold: 0
    non_blocking:
      - name: code_coverage
        check: coverage_percent
        threshold: 80           # warn if below 80%, do not block

  staging-to-production:
    blocking:
      - name: eval_pass_rate
        check: eval_suite_pass_rate
        threshold: 0.90
        max_regression_vs_baseline: 0.02
      - name: latency_sla
        check: p95_latency_seconds
        threshold: 3.0
      - name: prompt_injection
        check: adversarial_pass_rate
        threshold: 0.99
    requires_manual_approval: true
    approval_sla_hours: 4
    min_approvers: 2

A customer support agent team ships two or three prompt updates per week. Without gates, a change that shifted the model's refusal rate from 3% to 18% reached production and created a surge of escalated tickets before being noticed. After implementing a staging eval gate requiring pass rate ≥ 90% and regression ≤ 2% versus the prior production baseline, the same type of regression was caught on the next occurrence at staging, four days before the change would have gone live. The gate's eval report showed the refusal-rate metric specifically, allowing the team to identify and fix the system prompt instruction that caused the shift within two hours.
Gating on metrics you do not track.
A gate that checks "quality" against a metric not yet instrumented always passes vacuously. Gates must reference metrics with real measurement pipelines behind them.
Gating only on eval pass rate misses latency regressions, security issues, and tool-call reliability degradation. Use multi-dimensional gate configurations.
Treating bypass as a normal path.
Teams that routinely bypass gates to meet deadlines accumulate unreviewed risk. Bypass must require an explicit override comment and a post-incident review ticket.
A threshold set during project inception may be too loose twelve months later when the eval suite has grown and the model is more capable. Review thresholds quarterly.
The regression check compares the candidate to the current production artifact. If the production baseline is never updated, the regression budget accumulates across many releases.
Store gate configuration in version control.
Changes to thresholds go through the same PR review process as code changes. This prevents silently lowering a gate to let a failing build through.
Unit tests and static analysis should complete within 3–5 minutes. Reserve the expensive eval suite for the staging boundary.
Attach gate results to the artifact.
Store the full gate result JSON — including metric values, thresholds at check time, and timestamp — as artifact metadata. This supports retrospective analysis.
If the same gate blocks three consecutive builds, page the on-call engineer. Repeated failures may indicate a broken test environment, not a code regression.
Run gates against the artifact, not the source.
Gates that rebuild from source at gate time defeat the purpose; they may produce a different binary. Always evaluate the exact artifact that will be deployed.

between countries. The artifact is a traveler carrying documentation (eval results, security reports, build metadata). Each border requires different documents and checks: the first border (dev to staging) is a routine passport check; the last border (staging to production) demands a full customs inspection plus possibly a visa from a human approver. A traveler who clears all checkpoints arrives at the destination with a traceable, verified record of every check they passed. One who is turned back at any checkpoint cannot proceed until the issue is resolved.

The key insight is that the traveler (artifact) does not change between checkpoints. The checks may be progressively more thorough, but the entity being checked is identical throughout the journey. This is what distinguishes a promotion gate pipeline from ad-hoc testing: it is the
, verified at every boundary, not a fresh build that might differ from what was tested.

At scale, eval gates become the dominant pipeline cost. A 500-case eval suite at an average of 2 API calls per case costs approximately 1,000 LLM calls per promotion attempt. With five engineers each deploying once per day, this is 5,000 LLM calls per day from gate evals alone. Caching eval results for identical artifact-plus-eval-set combinations eliminates redundant calls; semantic caching (reusing results for inputs within a distance threshold of a prior cached input) reduces costs further at the risk of missing subtle regressions.
across multiple CI workers to reduce wall-clock time. A 500-case suite can run in under 5 minutes with 50 parallel workers, each handling 10 cases.
Run the 50 most discriminating cases at the dev boundary for speed, then run the full 500-case suite only at staging. Fast feedback for trivial failures; thorough coverage for production promotions.
If the artifact digest has not changed between two pipeline runs (a re-run due to transient CI failure), reuse prior gate results rather than re-executing the full suite.
Native gate support via required status checks and environment protection rules. Manual approval gates are configured per environment in the GitHub UI.
Provides configurable numeric thresholds and blocking decisions. The Input step enables human-in-the-loop approval.
Built-in automated canary analysis (Kayenta) and deployment pipeline gates with metric-based judgments.
Produce structured JSON output suitable for reading by a gate runner script.
Container image vulnerability scanners that output machine-readable JSON; easily integrated as gate checks.
Gate results must reference artifact digests, not tags.
A mutable image tag can be overwritten between the gate check and the deploy. Always record and verify the SHA256 digest to ensure the deployed artifact is the one that passed the gate.
Log bypass events at a higher severity.
Bypasses must appear in the security audit log with approver identity and justification. Unapproved bypass must be impossible — enforce this via branch protection rules and required reviews.
The script that evaluates gates must be version-controlled and reviewed. A compromised gate runner that always exits zero provides no protection.
If gate results can be written by any pipeline job, they can be fabricated. Write results to an append-only store with IAM policies restricting writes to the gate runner job identity.
A promotion gate is an automated quality checkpoint that must pass before an artifact advances between environments; it evaluates the same binary at each boundary rather than rebuilding.
For agentic AI, gates must cover eval pass rate, latency, security scans, and prompt injection resistance — not just unit test pass rate.
Define gates as code in version control; threshold changes require the same PR review as production code changes.
Tier gate suites by environment: fast automated checks at the dev boundary, expensive eval suites only at the staging-to-production boundary.
Store gate results alongside the artifact digest for auditability; every production deployment should have a traceable record of which gates it passed and at what values.
Bypass events must be logged with approver identity and justification; normalize bypass as an exceptional rather than routine action.
Gate on regression versus baseline (e.g., eval pass rate must not drop more than 2% from current production) in addition to absolute thresholds, to catch gradual degradation.
